nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving streams, marshes, and rice paddies of South and Southeast Asia. Submersed growth produces fine needle-like leaves arranged in dense whorls around slender stems; emersed leaves are smaller and less colorful.

decorPreferences: HIGH light (PAR 50+) is required to drive pink/red/orange/yellow tip coloration
Reliable CO2 injection (20–30 ppm) strongly recommended for color and to prevent legginess
Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–7.0) intensifies red/pink tones
Iron-rich fertilization (EI or PMDD) drives the brightest coloration
Plant in dense clusters of multiple stems for a full bushy effect
Good water flow prevents debris accumulating in the fine needle-like foliage
Trim tops regularly and replant healthy cuttings to keep the bush dense and prevent shading-out of lower stems

summary: An Asian background stem plant prized for its fine needle-like leaves arranged in tight whorls and its brilliant pink-to-red tip coloration under high-tech conditions. Demanding: requires high light, CO2 injection, iron-rich ferts, and soft acidic water to express color and avoid legginess.
careNotes:
Rotala wallichii is a classic high-tech background stem plant native to South and Southeast Asia. Its distinguishing trait is the fine, needle-like submersed foliage arranged in whorls around slim stems — a very different look from the broader-leaved R. rotundifolia or R. macrandra. Naturally green, the tips flush pink, red, orange, and yellow under strong lighting, reliable CO2 injection, and a well-balanced fertilization regime emphasizing iron.

Care is advanced. The plant melts readily under low CO2, ammonia spikes, or sudden parameter swings, and goes leggy (sparse internodes, dropped lower leaves) under insufficient light. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–7.0, low gH/kH) brings out the strongest red/pink coloration; harder, alkaline water pushes color back to greenish-bronze. Plant in dense clusters of multiple stems and trim aggressively at the top to maintain a full bushy hedge; healthy cuttings can be replanted as the primary propagation method.

Handle gently — the fine leaves are fragile and bruise easily. Provide good water flow through the planting to keep debris from accumulating in the foliage.

Cross-references: fw-plant-046 (R. rotundifolia), fw-plant-081 (R. indica), fw-plant-160 ('Colorata'), fw-plant-161 ('H'Ra'), fw-plant-162 (R. macrandra).

primary.name: Buce Plant — Rotala Wallichii
primary.notes: Family Lythraceae; SE Asia native; pH 6–7.5; moderate-to-high light; CO2 recommended; fast growth; background placement; pink/red/orange/yellow coloration under high light + CO2 + ferts; fragile leaves; melts under parameter swings.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium — Rotala Wallichii
additional.notes: Whorly Rotala common name; SE Asia native; 64–82°F (~18–28°C); moderate-to-strong light; CO2 highly recommended for color; fast growth; midground/background; thin needle-like leaves in whorls; greenish-bronze to red/pink tips under strong light.
